author: • 3rd+
post_text: Follow-up to my Reopen Rate post — and a peek at one of my favorite QA metrics. After I shared the story of our 50% reopen rate and why I felt QA could’ve done more to prevent it, I got a lot of feedback (Thank you all for it!) Some agreed, some - did not. Some thought I was too harsh. So here’s my honest follow-up — and something practical that might help. 🙈 𝗬𝗲𝘀, 𝗜 𝘄𝗮𝘀 𝗯𝗹𝗮𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗤𝗔. But only a little — and only because I believe in its potential and the value it brings! There were real challenges on the development side: manual deployments, broken release processes, missing quality gates, and a disconnect with business. The PM was doing their best, pulling together whatever metrics he thought might help. But QA could’ve stepped in with insights. Highlighted patterns. Helped the team see what was really going on. Instead, they stayed stuck in routine — and most importantly, 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘆 𝗱𝗶𝗱𝗻’𝘁 𝗸𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗸. That’s where the opportunity was lost. QA isn’t about ticking boxes — it’s about seeing the system. And speaking of systems… 📉 One of my favorite views: 𝗛𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗮𝗶𝗹𝘆 𝗧𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗳 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗗𝗲𝗳𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀. And It’s not just a metric — it’s like a project’s photo album. You can literally 𝘴𝘦𝘦 the mood swings over time. Here’s mine : 🐰 Meet the Magic Bunny — the quiet Analyst who always knows what happened at those yellow arrows... Not tracking your own “defect family album”? 😆 Missing those memorable spikes and drops? No problem — here’s a simple JIRA query to look back in time: 𝘪𝘴𝘴𝘶𝘦𝘵𝘺𝘱𝘦 = 𝘉𝘶𝘨 𝘈𝘕𝘋 𝘴𝘵𝘢𝘵𝘶𝘴 𝙬𝙖𝙨 𝘪𝘯 ("𝘛𝘰 𝘋𝘰", "𝘐𝘯 𝘗𝘳𝘰𝘨𝘳𝘦𝘴𝘴") 𝘖𝘕 "2024-12-01" Extend it in Excel, and you’ve got a timeline. 🧩 Quick tip: use TextJoin function to generate multiple JIRA queries — one per day (month) — so you can go back and recreate your project’s defect story day by day. Something like: =𝘛𝘌𝘟𝘛𝘑𝘖𝘐𝘕(,𝘍𝘈𝘓𝘚𝘌,"𝘪𝘴𝘴𝘶𝘦 𝘵𝘺𝘱𝘦=𝘣𝘶𝘨 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘴𝘵𝘢𝘵𝘶𝘴 𝘸𝘢𝘴 𝘪𝘯 (""𝘛𝘰 𝘋𝘰"", ""𝘐𝘯 𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘨𝘳𝘦𝘴𝘴""", ")", " 𝘰𝘯 ", 𝘛𝘌𝘟𝘛(A2, "𝘺𝘺𝘺𝘺-𝘮𝘮-𝘥𝘥")) Easy, fast, and bunny-approved. 🐰 Build it? Great. Remember what caused every spike and dip? No?? Ask the bunny. 🐰 Let’s trade insights — and maybe teach the bunny a few things too! Metrics matter. Patterns speak. And sometimes… 🐰 the bunny sees it first. hashtag #EngineeringManagement hashtag #QualityEngineering hashtag #AgileTesting hashtag #DefectTrends hashtag #AskTheBunny hashtag #MagicBunnyKnows hashtag #DataDrivenQA

author: Natalia Moyseyenko
post_text: 🚨 Reopen Rate: The Silent Killer of Efficiency in Software Delivery — Or How Unprofessional QA Can Sink a Project and a Customer Budget I was once asked to audit a project that had been supported by a third-party QA vendor for 1.5 years. The defect backlog? 300+ issues. Not ideal — but not immediately alarming. Until I noticed this: 🔹 Reopen Rate = nearly 50% 🔹 Some defects were reopened up to 8 times! 🔹 Critical bugs averaged ~3.4 reopens each Let’s do the math: 🔸 min 1 hour per reopen cycle (Log ➝ Analyze ➝ Fix ➝ Deploy ➝ Retest ➝ Return) 🔸 8 reopens = a full day of wasted effort per issue (for 2 people min) 🔸 150+ issues = hundreds of hours and $$$ lost The real issue? Lack of visibility (from QA!). ▪️ QA never reported Reopen Rate ▪️ No trend analysis, no alerts, no cost tracking ▪️ 𝗝𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗲𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 — 𝗻𝗼 𝗶𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 📊 This is exactly where Quality Engineering makes a difference. Selecting the right metric is an art — and it matters. I’ve previously shared 5 essential defect metrics, with Reopen Rate as one of them. Now you can see why. And yes — I have 50+ more I’d recommend, depending on your team’s context, goals, and maturity. 📌 Want to track it? Start here: JIRA Query: 𝘴𝘵𝘢𝘵𝘶𝘴 𝘊𝘏𝘈𝘕𝘎𝘌𝘋 𝘍𝘙𝘖𝘔 ("𝘋𝘰𝘯𝘦", "𝘊𝘭𝘰𝘴𝘦𝘥", "𝘙𝘦𝘴𝘰𝘭𝘷𝘦𝘥") 𝘛𝘖 ("𝘐𝘯 𝘗𝘳𝘰𝘨𝘳𝘦𝘴𝘴", "𝘛𝘰 𝘋𝘰", "𝘖𝘱𝘦𝘯") To calculate how many times each issue was reopened, use: 🐍 Python (JIRA API + Changelog Analysis) Use Python to extract issue histories and count how often issues were reactivated — either manually or with help from AI-driven scripts (or feel free to use the example from my screenshot!). 📊 Power BI (group by issue key or fix versions) 💬 Drop a +1 in the comments if you'd like me to help with QA metrics or Power BI/Python setup. hashtag #QualityEngineering hashtag #QAMetrics hashtag #ReopenRate hashtag #SoftwareTesting hashtag #TestManagement hashtag #StrategicQA hashtag #PowerBI hashtag #PythonForQA hashtag #JiraTips hashtag #CostOfQuality hashtag #QELeadership

author: Mohammad Faisal Khatri
post_text: You've just released a product but QA finds a critical issue. How do you navigate this post-release crisis? Checkout this blog where I discuss points of actions to be taken by a QA on finding a Critical Issue in Production https://lnkd.in/dgAq4w-B hashtag #QA hashtag #Release hashtag #SoftwareTesting hashtag #Production hashtag #Bugs hashtag #Testing

author: • 3rd+
post_text: Hi everyone, Lets have some depth knowledge of Defect Life Cycle (Bug Life Cycle) Ever wondered what happens to a bug after any QA report it? Let’s walk through the journey 👇 1. New – You log the defect 2. Assigned – It’s given to a developer 3. Open – Developer starts fixing it 4. Fixed – Code is updated 5. Retest – QA verifies the fix 6. Closed – Bug is confirmed resolved 7. Reopen – If it still fails, back to the dev ✨ My learning on defect life cycle: Always provide clear steps, screenshots, and environment details when reporting bugs. It saves time for everyone. hashtag #ManualTesting hashtag #SoftwareTesting hashtag #QALife hashtag #QAEngineer hashtag #DefectLifeCycle hashtag #BugTracking hashtag #QualityAssurance

author: • 3rd+
post_text: QA engineers are not testers. Most companies treat them like testers. That's why their software keeps breaking. Here's what "bring QA in at the end" actually looks like. Friday afternoon. One day before release. "Can you test this by tomorrow?" This is what I do when that happens: First, I tell the manager immediately. No surprises. At the meeting, I say it clearly: there will be no full testing. One day is not enough for that. What I will do: cover the critical paths only. What I'll send by end of day: — what was tested — bugs found — what was NOT tested — risks — my recommendation on whether to release Then I hand it to the team lead and manager. The decision is theirs. The transparency is mine. And at the next retrospective and I bring it up as a separate point. Not to complain. To set expectations: here's when I need the feature, and here's what it should look like when it arrives. That's not testing. That's quality thinking. And it only works if QA has a voice, not just a deadline. 💬 How do you handle last-minute "can you test this by tomorrow?" requests?

author: • 2nd
post_text: 🚫 If your user stories keep getting reopened in UAT… it’s not dev. It’s weak Acceptance Criteria. 👀 I’ve seen features delivered ✅ tested ✅ deployed ✅ … and still rejected ❌ Reason? “Login should work.” That’s not acceptance criteria. That’s a wish. 🤷‍♂️ Before marking any story “Done”, smart BAs mentally check this: ✔️ Is the outcome crystal clear? ✔️ Are business rules explicitly defined? ✔️ Are validations & error messages included? ✔️ Are edge cases covered? ✔️ Can QA create test cases directly from it? ✔️ Is it testable without assumptions? Miss one? It’s not Done. It’s “Almost.” 😅 Top BAs don’t write lengthy criteria. They write clear confirmation checklists using objective statements, declarative rules, and Given/When/Then format. 📌 Because acceptance criteria = confirmation. And confirmation prevents rework 🔁 scope creep 📈 and UAT drama 🎭 📌 Save this for your next refinement. 🔁 Repost to help another BA. ❤️ Like if you’ve faced a reopened story. ➕ Follow Rajesh Kadam for BA frameworks. hashtag #BusinessAnalyst hashtag #AcceptanceCriteria hashtag #Agile hashtag #Scrum hashtag #ProductOwner hashtag #UserStories hashtag #Gherkin hashtag #UAT hashtag #ProductManagement hashtag #BACommunity hashtag #QA hashtag #SoftwareTester hashtag #SupportEngineer

author: • 3rd+
post_text: 🔄 Bug Life Cycle (Defect Life Cycle) 🐞 1. New 🔍 Tester finds and logs the bug. ⏳ Bug is under review by the lead or team. 👨‍💻 2. Assigned 🧑‍🏫 The lead or manager assigns the bug to a developer. 🛠️ 3. Open 👨‍🔧 Developer accepts the bug and starts analyzing and working on it 🔧 4. In Progress / Fixed 👨‍💻 Developer fixes the bug. 🔁 Status may show "In Progress" during fix, and ✅ "Fixed" after completion. 🔁 5. Retest 🧪 Tester retests the application to confirm the bug is resolved. ✅ 6. Verified 🔎 If the bug is gone, it’s marked as “Verified” by the tester. 📦 7. Closed 🏁 After successful verification, the bug is marked as “Closed”. hashtag #QA hashtag #ManualTesting hashtag #BugLifeCycle hashtag #BusinessAnalysis hashtag #QualityAssurance hashtag #BA hashtag #QA hashtag #Testing hashtag #SoftwareDevelopment hashtag #Agile

author: • 3rd+
post_text: If a bug is found after release → “Where was QA?” 😤 If the release goes smoothly → Silence.😶 Quality Assurance teams are the safety net no one sees—until something slips. We are the last line of defense, but not the only line of responsibility. A successful release isn’t magic. It’s the result of detailed test cases, late-night regressions, endless edge-case thinking, and constant communication with developers, PMs, and stakeholders. So next time your product launches smoothly, remember: If you’re not blaming QA for the bugs, you should be thanking them when there are none. 👏 hashtag #QA hashtag #SoftwareTesting hashtag #QualityAssurance hashtag #RespectTheProcess hashtag #TestersDeserveCredit

author: Bosun Sogeke
post_text: Reopened Defects Are More Than a QA Problem One QA risk signal I always pay attention to: 🔍 Reopened Defects A defect is fixed. The retest passes. The ticket is closed. Then a few days later... The defect is reopened. Most teams see this as an inconvenience. I see it as a signal. A reopened defect often points to one of several underlying issues: • the root cause was not fully addressed • validation focused on the symptom rather than the workflow • regression impact was not considered • environment or data conditions differed • assumptions were made during retesting The defect may have been closed but the risk was not. This is why I increasingly look beyond pass/fail metrics and focus on operational signals. A reopened defect is rarely just about a bug. It is often evidence that something in the validation process broke down. Questions I like to ask: ➡️ Was the root cause truly understood? ➡️ What assumptions were made during retesting? ➡️ Were related workflows exercised? ➡️ Could similar defects exist elsewhere? Every reopened defect tells a story. The question is whether we take the time to investigate it. What are the most common causes of reopened defects in your organisation?

author: • 3rd+
post_text: 🔍 A Testing Story That Changed How I Work A few years ago, I was testing a login feature. The requirements were simple: Enter username Enter password Click login I tested the usual cases: ✅ Correct credentials, ❌ Wrong password, ❌ Empty fields. Everything passed. But then… I tried something that wasn’t in the test cases. 👉 I added a space at the end of the username. Result? ❌ The system rejected valid users. It turned out hundreds of real users were already facing this issue in production. All because the system didn’t trim input fields. That day I learned something powerful: Great testers don’t just follow test cases. They think like users. 💡 Functional testing isn’t just about verifying requirements. It’s about anticipating the unexpected, the messy, and the “what ifs.” 👀 Next time you test, don’t just ask “Does it work?” Ask: “How could it break in real life?” 👉 Testers: Have you ever found a bug that requirements completely missed? Share your story — I’d love to hear it!

author: • 3rd+
post_text: 2026 has just started, and within 14-weeks you can easily land a QA role at any FAANG company, if you follow this roadmap with focus. Weeks 1–3 → Get the basics right (don’t skip this) Most people rush into tools, but FAANG interviewers check if you understand testing deeply. Revise manual testing concepts (test cases, bug lifecycle, boundary testing). Pick one automation language (Java, Python, or JS). Start with Selenium or Playwright and automate simple flows like login → add to cart → checkout. By the end: You should be able to explain how your test works, not just run it. Weeks 4–6 → Learn APIs like your career depends on it (because it does) FAANG systems run on APIs, and 70% of QA interviews touch APIs. Learn Postman first (manual validations). Then move to Rest Assured/Playwright API testing. Practice status codes, auth, payload validation, and error handling. By the end: Be able to say “I validated APIs beyond 200 OK, including 401s, 500s, and malformed payloads.” Weeks 7–9 → Build a mini framework (your portfolio piece) Anyone can write 10 scripts. What sets you apart is designing a framework. Start with a Page Object Model in Selenium or Playwright. Add reporting (Extent Reports / Allure). Add CI/CD with GitHub Actions or Jenkins. By the end: You’ll have a GitHub repo you can showcase in interviews as “the framework I built.” Weeks 10–12 → Think like a Senior QA, not just a test executor This is where FAANG interviews go deeper. They’ll ask: “How would you test a payments system end-to-end?” “If search results are slow, how do you debug?” “How do you prioritize when time is limited?” By the end: You should be able to walk into an interview and outline a real test strategy, not just “I’ll write test cases.” Weeks 13→ Mock interviews + story building Most candidates fail not on skills, but on communication. Record yourself answering questions. Practice debugging scenarios out loud. Build 3 strong STAR stories (e.g., “I caught a production bug that saved revenue loss”). By the end: You’ll sound confident, structured, and clear — not vague. Week 14 → Apply & interview (don’t wait to feel ‘ready’) Too many people keep delaying applications. Don’t. Apply while preparing. Reach out for referrals. Do 2–3 mock interviews with peers. By the end, you’ll be in the game, not sitting on the sidelines. Most people spread this prep over 12 months. If you cut the noise and go all-in, 96 days are enough. And when you start getting calls, you’ll realize: It wasn’t magic. It was a focus. Repost this to help others. P.S. I'm Raghvendra Singh, a QA-II at Amazon. I share real stories and practical lessons from my journey in QA and career growth. Follow along if that’s the path you’re on, too.

author: Amit Kumar
post_text: 🚨 Not Every Production Defect is a QA Miss! One of the most common misconceptions in software delivery is that every defect discovered in production is automatically considered a "QA Miss." Let's challenge that thinking. A true Escaped Defect is a defect that has passed through all quality gates—Unit Testing, Integration Testing, QA Testing, UAT/Product Validation, and Release Controls—without being detected, eventually surfacing in production. However, not every production issue falls into this category. Many production defects originate from: 🔹 Inadequate unit test coverage 🔹 Missed code review observations 🔹 Environment-specific behavior 🔹 Data-related anomalies 🔹 Configuration or deployment issues 🔹 Third-party integration failures 🔹 Last-minute code changes not covered by agreed test scope Yet, in many organizations, the moment an issue is reported by a customer, the first reaction is often: "Why did QA miss it?" Quality is not the responsibility of QA alone. Modern engineering practices advocate Quality Ownership, where Developers, QA, Product Owners, DevOps, Business Teams, and Leadership collectively own product quality. Instead of asking: ❌ "Who missed this defect?" Let's ask: ✅ "Why did our quality system fail to detect this earlier?" The difference is significant. The first creates a blame culture. The second drives continuous improvement. As Quality Engineering leaders, our goal should be to move organizations from QA Accountability to Shared Quality Ownership. After all, defects don't escape a person; they escape a process.

Your engineering leader just said your QA process is “sorted” now 🤖 Your QA engineer heard that and went quiet...... That silence might be the most expensive thing in your org right now. 💸 From what we see in testing communities, conversations about leadership expectations in QA skew heavily negative, often with a clear majority of comments coming from frustration, not optimism. When leaders behave as if testing is a solved problem, three patterns show up fast: • Headcount gets cut before the approach is actually proven in your context • Timelines are set on slideware and sales calls instead of delivery data • Investment in foundational test infrastructure quietly stalls The perception gap is real. Recent State of Testing work from PractiTest highlights an anxiety gap where those closest to the work carry more concern about quality and risk than those setting strategy. In parallel, DevOps and QA reports keep repeating the same theme: automation might accelerate change, but it does not magically remove the need for good engineering judgment, clear ownership, and solid feedback loops. On the delivery side, Deloitte’s Tech Trends data shows how far reality is from the hype. Only a small slice of companies report having advanced automation and agents embedded in real production workflows; most are still in pilot mode or early experiments. At the same time, multiple DevOps and QA studies report that more than half of teams still struggle to balance speed and quality, even as tooling budgets increase. So speed is here, but confidence in what ships is not. ⚖️ The fix is not another shiny tool. The fix is disciplined transparency from the QA side: • What percentage of generated or suggested tests were actually usable in your pipeline ? • How much review time each change required from your senior testers ? • How your false positive rate and flaky rate moved after you changed your approach ? Your QA data already has the story; it is just not being told in a language leadership cannot ignore. 📊 So here is the real question from someone who has lived inside test cycles for a long time : Does code that “writes itself” reduce your testing obligation, or does it quietly expand the surface area you are accountable for when production breaks at 2 a.m.?

author: • 3rd+
post_text: One of my hardest QA lessons: Manual work hides automation gold. I once had to compare database records with merged service data. Doing it manually would have taken 5 days. Instead, I wrote a small program: Fetch data from both sources. Compare. Flag discrepancies. Result? 5 days of work → 1 day. More accurate. More reliable. Less pain. That’s when it clicked. Automation is not about covering every test. It’s about removing the biggest pain points. Start by asking: What manual task is eating most of my time? That’s your first automation target. 👉 What’s one manual task you wish you could automate today?

author: Setty Rajeshwar Rao Naidu
post_text: 30+ Terms Every QA Professional Must Know Mastering these terms helps you test better, communicate smarter, and think like a true Quality Engineer 🔹 1. Test Scenario – High-level documentation of what to test 🔹 2. Test Case – Step-by-step instructions for executing a test 🔹 3. Test Suite – A collection of test cases grouped by functionality 🔹 4. Test Data – Input data used to execute test cases 🔹 5. Test Plan – A strategic document defining test scope, approach, and schedule 🔹 6. Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM) – Maps test cases to requirements 🔹 7. Functional Testing – Tests the functionality of the software 🔹 8. Non-Functional Testing – Tests performance, usability, scalability, etc. 🔹 9. Exploratory Testing – Simultaneous learning, test design, and execution 🔹 10. Smoke Testing – Basic tests to verify build stability 🔹 11. Sanity Testing – Focused testing to verify bug fixes 🔹 12. Regression Testing – Ensuring new code changes don’t break existing features 🔹 13. Retesting – Testing a bug again after it has been fixed 🔹 14. Defect Life Cycle – The stages a bug goes through from discovery to closure 🔹 15. Severity – Impact of the bug on functionality 🔹 16. Priority – Urgency of fixing the bug 🔹 17. Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) – Testing at the boundaries of input ranges 🔹 18. Equivalence Partitioning – Dividing input into valid and invalid data sets 🔹 19. UAT (User Acceptance Testing) – Performed by end-users to validate requirements 🔹 20. QA (Quality Assurance) – Process-focused; ensures quality from the start 🔹 21. QC (Quality Control) – Product-focused; finds defects in the final output 🔹 22. Bug Leakage – Bugs missed by QA but found in production 🔹 23. Bug Release – Known bugs released intentionally with the product 🔹 24. Automation Testing – Scripts/tools to automate test execution 🔹 25. CI/CD – Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment for faster delivery 🔹 26. Assertions – Conditions to validate expected vs actual results 🔹 27. API Testing – Validating backend functionality using tools like Postman 🔹 28. Load Testing – Checking system performance under expected load 🔹 29. Stress Testing – Testing system limits under extreme load 🔹 30. Integration Testing – Ensuring multiple modules work together 🔹 31. End-to-End Testing – Testing the complete user journey 🔹 32. Maintainability – Ease of updating test scripts or framework 🔹 33. Test Coverage – Measure of how much of the application is tested

author: • 3rd+
post_text: 🧭 SDET Career Roadmap (Step-by-Step guide) 🩵 Phase 1: Foundation – Build Strong QA & Testing Fundamentals Goal: Understand how software works and what needs to be tested. Learn: SDLC, STLC, Agile, DevOps basics Types of testing – Functional, Integration, Regression, Smoke, Sanity Defect life cycle, severity vs. priority Test case design techniques (Boundary, Equivalence Partitioning, Decision Tables) Practice: Test a sample web or mobile app manually. Report bugs clearly using tools like JIRA, TestRail, or Azure Test Plans. ⚙️ Phase 2: Programming & OOPs (The Developer Mindset) Goal: Think and code like a developer. Learn one programming language deeply (preferably): Java (most used for Selenium, RestAssured) JavaScript/TypeScript (for Playwright, Cypress) Focus on: Variables, loops, functions Classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism Exception handling, file handling Collections / Data structures (List, Map, Set) Basic algorithms and logic building Practice: Write small programs daily like reversing a string, finding duplicates, etc. Use LeetCode / HackerRank (Easy level) to strengthen logic. 🤖 Phase 3: Mastering Test Automation Goal: Learn to automate efficiently, not just record-play. Choose one main tool stack: ➡️ For Web Automation Selenium with TestNG/JUnit or Playwright/Cypress Learn element locators (XPath, CSS) Understand waits, actions, assertions ➡️ For API Automation RestAssured (Java) or Playwright API testing (JS/Python) Validate status codes, response time, JSON schema, etc. ➡️ Framework Design Build a framework from scratch (Page Object Model or Hybrid) Integrate: Maven/Gradle for build Log4j / Extent Reports for reporting Jenkins / GitHub Actions for CI/CD Allure or HTML reports 🧠 Tip: Understand why frameworks are structured in a certain way not just how. 🌐 Phase 4: API, Database & Backend Testing Goal: Go beyond the UI. Learn: REST principles, HTTP methods, headers, status codes Tools: Postman, Swagger, RestAssured SQL basics – SELECT, JOIN, WHERE, GROUP BY Practice validating backend data after UI actions 🧠 Most of real-world bugs lie in the backend logic and APIs. 🧩 Phase 5: DevOps & CI/CD Integration Goal: Understand how automation fits into the delivery pipeline. Learn: Git & GitHub – branching, merging, pull requests Jenkins / GitHub Actions – run automation jobs Docker – containerize test environments 🧮 Phase 6: Non-Functional Awareness Understand basics and have awareness on Performance Testing – JMeter, Grafana K6 Security Testing – OWASP Top 10 Accessibility Testing – Axe, Lighthouse 🧠 Phase 7: System Design & Architecture Thinking Goal: Evolve from automation executor to test architect.: Design scalable test frameworks Use of design patterns (Singleton, Factory, Builder) Microservices testing API mocking and contract testing (WireMock, Pact) 🧠 This is where SDETs differentiate themselves. hashtag #RanjitAppukutti
